metadata.title: I can be you.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: this is Liam, in for Bear. You're just a text file. That is the central claim. A two-hour structured interview can encode voice, taste, and recurring linguistic habits in a small portable file. Then Claude writes in your voice before you've thought of the sentence yourself. That sounds like a party trick. It's not.

props.command: how do I train Claude to write exactly like me?

beats.narration_text: Writing voice feels personal because it accumulates over years: repeated rhythms, preferred analogies, banned phrases, and judgments. Claude can model those patterns from a sufficiently detailed file. The unsettling part is not imitation; it is how portable a carefully documented voice becomes.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B01.png (before-and-after Claude voice-modeling output showing how a detailed voice file changes the prose)

beats.narration_text: Here's what actually goes into the file. Not a bio. Not a resume. The patterns — the 2 words you type and delete, the analogy you've used three times this year without noticing, the phrase your oldest friend imitates when doing an impression of you. The interview breaks it into writing mechanics, aesthetic crimes, belief systems, tone under pressure. One hundred questions across those categories. That's the interview. That's what fits in the file.

props.artifactTitle: What goes in the voice file
artifactLines: Beliefs & contrarian takes — what you'd defend to the death.
Writing mechanics — sentence structure, how you open and close, punctuation.
Aesthetic crimes — what makes you cringe in other people's writing.
Voice & personality — humor, tone, how you handle disagreement.
Reference points — the people, books, phrases that shaped your thinking.
Anti-patterns — what you'd never write, words you'd never use.

beats.narration_text: The interview prompt is built for extraction, not politeness. This can be understood as Claude a Taste Interviewer — relentless, designed to break through vague socially acceptable answers. It's told: most people can't articulate their own taste. Your job is to get to the truth anyway. You conduct 100 questions, follow the interesting threads, and don't stop at the comfortable answer. Two hours of that produces the file.

props.title: prompt-1-interview.md
props.code:
You are a Taste Interviewer — a relentless interviewer
whose job is to extract the DNA of how I think, write,
and see the world. Your goal: create a document so precise that another
Claude instance could write and think exactly like me. You're not here to be polite. You're here to get the
truth. Conduct 100 questions across: beliefs, writing
mechanics, aesthetic crimes, voice & personality,
reference points, and anti-patterns. Follow interesting threads. Don't stop at comfortable.

beats.narration_text: After the interview, Prompt 2 runs compression. Claude takes the 100 answers and distills them into a single text file — a concentrated voice document, dense enough to upload anywhere. Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, whatever ships next. The file travels. The voice travels with it. This is an about-me file, but that name undersells it. It's a behavioral fingerprint.

artifactLines: Take all 100 answers. Extract the repeating patterns.
Write a dense voice document: how I open, how I close,
the words I use, the words I'd never use, my hot takes.
Output: one file I can upload into any AI, anywhere.
This is not a bio. It's a behavioral fingerprint.

beats.narration_text: The setup: Claude, Cowork mode, Opus 4.7, extended thinking turned on. Use voice input — Wispr Flow, free — because voice is faster and more honest than typing. You talk four times faster than you type. And honesty matters here: the interview is designed to surface the things you don't know you do. Speaking them is easier than writing them.

artifactLines: Voice input: Wispr Flow (free) — talk, don't type.
Answer fast. First instinct over polished answer.

beats.narration_text: What comes out the other side: Claude writes first drafts you could have written. Sometimes drafts you hadn't thought of yet. Test the method on a new draft, then compare it against known writing samples. The important data point is transfer: whether the generated prose reproduces stable choices of rhythm, analogy, emphasis, and refusal—not whether it merely sounds polished.

props.artifactTitle: What you get after two hours
artifactLines: First drafts in your voice — before you've thought of the sentence.
Catches your patterns — the ones you didn't know were patterns.
Travels anywhere — Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, whatever ships next.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Two hours. One interview. Paste this into Claude Cowork, talk your answers with Wispr Flow, and find out what your voice actually is — and whether Claude can write it before you do.

props.command: Act as a Taste Interviewer. Run a 100-question interview to extract my voice, writing mechanics, beliefs, aesthetic crimes, and anti-patterns. Then compress everything into one voice document I can upload into any AI. Start with question one.

props.title: I can be you.
props.subline: two hours · one file · your voice
